title: Quiz: Lattice Surgery Operations
description: Test your understanding of lattice surgery techniques for implementing logical gates on surface codes

questions.question: What is lattice surgery in the context of surface codes?

options.text: A technique for physically cutting quantum chips
A method of implementing logical gates by merging and splitting code patches
A way to measure physical qubit states

questions.explanation: Lattice surgery is a technique for implementing logical operations on surface codes by physically merging code patches (to measure joint Paulis) and splitting them back apart. This approach is preferred over defect braiding because it has lower overhead and is more compatible with planar qubit architectures.

questions.question: When two surface code patches with 'rough' boundaries are merged, what Pauli operator is measured?
questions.explanation: Rough boundaries support X-type logical operators, so merging two rough boundaries measures the joint XX operator. Conversely, smooth boundaries support Z-type operators, and merging smooth boundaries measures ZZ. This boundary-operator correspondence is fundamental to lattice surgery.

questions.question: How is a CNOT gate implemented via lattice surgery?

options.text: By directly applying physical CNOT gates to all qubits
By measuring XX between control and ancilla, ZZ between ancilla and target, then applying Pauli corrections
By braiding defects around each other
By teleporting the control qubit to the target location

questions.explanation: CNOT via lattice surgery uses the measurement-based approach: (1) Prepare ancilla in |+>, (2) Measure XX between control and ancilla, (3) Measure ZZ between ancilla and target, (4) Apply Pauli corrections S^m_XX and Z^m_ZZ based on measurement outcomes. This requires about 6d code cycles.

questions.question: For a surface code with distance d, how many code cycles does a lattice surgery CNOT approximately take?
questions.explanation: A CNOT via lattice surgery takes approximately 6d code cycles: preparation (d), XX measurement/merge (d), XX split (d), ZZ measurement/merge (d), ZZ split (d), and final ancilla measurement (d). Each step requires d cycles to reliably extract the syndrome.

questions.question: What is the main challenge when performing lattice surgery between non-adjacent logical qubits?

options.text: Routing is needed, requiring ancilla chains or qubit movement

questions.explanation: Lattice surgery requires physical adjacency of patches. For non-adjacent qubits, we must either: (1) use intermediate ancilla patches to create a chain connecting them, (2) perform swap operations to move qubits closer, or (3) design the layout to place frequently-interacting qubits adjacent. Routing adds significant overhead.

questions.question: What is the space-time volume scaling for a single lattice surgery CNOT on a distance-d code?
questions.explanation: Space-time volume = physical qubits x time. CNOT requires O(d^2) ancilla qubits (one logical qubit patch) and O(d) time cycles. The product is O(d^3). This volume metric is crucial for comparing different fault-tolerant implementations.

questions.question: Can multiple independent CNOTs be performed in parallel using lattice surgery?

options.text: No, all CNOTs must be sequential
Yes, CNOTs on disjoint qubit sets can run simultaneously
Only if they share a control qubit

questions.explanation: CNOTs on disjoint sets of qubits can be performed in parallel because they use separate patches and ancillas. This parallelism is one of the key advantages of lattice surgery. The total time for N parallel CNOTs is the same as for a single CNOT, providing up to Nx speedup.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of lattice surgery over defect braiding for surface codes?

options.text: Lattice surgery works with any qubit technology
Lattice surgery has lower overhead and is planar-compatible
Lattice surgery doesn't require error correction
Lattice surgery can implement T gates transversally

questions.explanation: Lattice surgery has several advantages over defect braiding: (1) lower qubit overhead since it doesn't need to maintain large defect regions, (2) planar compatibility - works with 2D qubit arrays without requiring non-planar connections, (3) more efficient multi-qubit measurements. Defect braiding requires more complex topological manipulations.
